sentences.text: I understand there's no editing done to these.
Yes.
My name is Warren Kahn, W-A-R-R-E-N K-A-H-N.
I was born in Wiesbaden, Germany, and my name actually was Werner at the time.
I-- I was-- that was my given name, W-E-R-N-E-R.
Later on when I was in the service and became a citizen, I had the opportunity to change my name to anglicize it to-- I picked Warren while I was at it.
I was born May 16th, 1924, and at the present, that would make me 72.
My father's name was Arnold, A-R-N-O-L-D, and he was born June 1st, 1890.
In Germany he was a lawyer, an attorney.
Yes, he had his own practice.
Actually, it was a partnership with another lawyer.
I think he was just a general attorney and notar also, which is a notary public.
Generally, these things went together in Germany.
Here you can-- in the United States, you can get a notary public at just about any drug store or post office, but in Germany at the time, that was handled by lawyers.
I can only tell you about his mother.
His father died when my father was just very young.
My father actually didn't know his father, doesn't remember his father at all.
And he was brought up by his mother.
Her name was Hedwig Kahn, H-E-D-W-I-G.
I remember her as a very patrician lady, very proper.
She never remarried, by the way, and she brought up my father and his sister very strictly and properly.
And I think his upbringing later on made him also a very proper and upright and ethical person.
And he was-- my father was known as a very ethical and good human being.
He liked to play cards and socialize.
He belonged to all kinds of organizations and was an officer in a few of them.
Otherwise, I don't know of any hobbies as such.
But he always was very active.
He did like the theater, and concerts and cultural things and he liked to read.
But as such, I don't remember him having any other hobbies.
My mother was born in the city of Triere, T-R-I-E-R.
That's on the Moselle River.
It's a very beautiful old Roman city.
And I remember that city very well.
Often, I visited my grandparents, who also lived in that town.
But my mother comes from that city.
And she-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was-- during World War I, she was a nurse's aid, a volunteer nurse's aid.
She was just a teenager at the time.
And she actually never had a job as such.
She wasn't trained for anything.
I remember my father always made fun of her because she couldn't add [LAUGHS] two numbers together.
Mathematics was not her favorite subject.
But she was-- she did a lot of reading and she was a devout pacifist politically, very much against any kind of war or-- and she tried to instill that into me, as well.
As a young child, I remember her telling about the futility and that wars should-- should not be and that also she-- the-- I remember her subscribing to pacifist literature.
She was also very sociable, a social butterfly more or less, very good-natured and very wonderful sense of humor and a very warm person and a very giving person.
Her parents are-- come from Triere and she was brought up very strictly by her parents.
Her father was a director of a bank, a really self-made man.
He came from very humble background, from some little village somewhere in the Eifel.
And I believe his father was a cattle dealer.
And he started to work at a bank and worked himself up as a manager or assistant manager of the branch of the Glasner Bank, which is a leading chain-- leading bank in Germany.
And then he became independent and became a-- he formed his own private bank and it was called [GERMAN], the Private Bank of Triere.
And it occupied the lower floor of the house where they lived.
And I remember very well as a child when I visited them, I played in the office and they had the high tables where the bookkeepers stood-- or-- or sat-- on high stools.
And I was allowed to play with the typewriter-- manual typewriters, of course, at that time.
And I had a good time.
My grandfather had his private office, which I was never to enter when he had someone there.
But my grandfather was very doting and when I was there in the office and there was no one there, he always came in and talked to me.
And he also was a very warm person.
I loved to go on walks with him.
And my grandfather also was a very devout religious person and he prayed twice a day and after each meal.
And he talked to me about the Jewish religion.
And one thing stood out in my mind very much.
He always said, as a Jew, you have to be-- work twice as hard, be twice as smart, and be twice as ethical as anyone else and then you can exist in Germany and do well in Germany.
But you always have to remember that.
And I think there is quite a bit of truth in that.
My grandmother, she was a very modern kind of person.
She was not as religious as my grandfather was, but she had a kosher household.
And she also took me places.
I remember once, it must have been around 1930, '31, somewhere in that time.
She took me to a movie and I remember I saw Mickey Mouse for the first time.
And I also saw a championship fight, Max Schmeling and Jack Sharkey.
I still remember that.
And since then, I became an avid fighter and I was pummeling her all the time, I'm fighting you; I'm Schmeling, you're Sharkey; and so on and so forth.
And she was a fun person.
And we also had [INAUDIBLE], and I always-- I once asked her, what is the most important thing as far as you're concerned?
She said, it surprised me, I like to be honored and recognized for what I am.
And I didn't go into it any further than that, but I thought it was interesting.
Do you feel like she was in her lifetime?
Was what?
She was respected.
I don't think she was active.
She was a housewife and she was well-liked among her friends, as far as I remember, her circle of friends.
And whether she was honored for anything, I don't know.
I think-- I think I may have-- I think I meant respected more than honored.
I think I expressed myself poorly there.
Yes.
My mother went to Wiesbaden to visit a relative, a cousin, and they went to a dance.
And my father was introduced to her at a dance.
And another funny thing is my grandfather always was a match-- tried to be a matchmaker.
And he put ads in the paper to get my mother married and try to get him-- to get her introduced to the proper person.
And of course, my mother would have absolutely nothing to do with it.
But when-- he-- when she met my father, they courted in secret for a while and then arranged for them to be introduced under the proper circumstances through someone that they both knew.
And from then on, my grandfather had no objections to my father.
In fact, they became very good friends shortly thereafter and they always got along famously.
In fact, all my family-- my other grandmother, also-- the family was very close and loving and there were never any bad situations that I was ever aware of.
Of course, as a child, I was somewhat sheltered, I may not have known about everything.
But I was not-- never made aware of anything of that sort.
I attended-- well, first I flunked out of kindergarten.
[LAUGHS] I was sent to some sort of a kindergarten and I must have behaved atrociously and was-- I think I flunked out.
I went there just a few days, as far as I remember.
I was very unhappy there and I was very happy not to have to go back there again.
And then I went to public school, the [GERMAN] in Wiesbaden.
The school itself was a very large school.
There were several first grades at the time.
I know-- I believe there were three first grades.
And my teacher was a Herr [?
Kanto, ?] a young teacher who came from Berlin.
And he was my teacher through all those four years of public school.
And he was, I believe, the greatest influence on my life, except for my parents.
He was very strict teacher and we often got the end of a-- a cane.
We were hit, physical punishment.
Today that would be called child abuse and would not be tolerated.
But at that time, it was considered part of the educational process and we never questioned it.
And we always assumed that being hit was part of our education.
And one thing, I always had an atrocious handwriting, I still do.
And he-- every time we signed a paper-- we-- we did a paper, he signed it with either a red C or a blue C.
And a red C it was passable and a-- a blue C was not acceptable.
Three blue Cs and you were marched up to the front, had to bend down, and you were hit quite hard with a cane.
And of course, other times, too-- if you misbehaved or neglected to do your homework, you were given physical punishment.
But aside from that, he was a wonderful teacher and a wonderful person and we learned a lot.
And he was a very kind person.
I was really not much of a scholar.
In the four years of elementary school, I did fairly well in just about all subjects.
I don't even recall any-- I mean, we had German, some German history, geography, things like that.
I don't recall too much of the actual curriculum in public school.
We had music-- we had a different teacher for music.
And understand that the other-- one of the other teachers that taught the first grade, the first four grades, was a devout Nazi, a really convinced Nazi.
But that time, that was still prior to the-- to Hitler and I never felt any kind of anti-Semitism or discrimination at all.
I understand that in one of those classes, the teacher did make remarks about Jews and some people became quite upset about it.
I had to walk to school and it was quite a long walk.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] well, to get into political-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] well, you-- I'll get into that a little later, I guess.
Yes.
I went to the [GERMAN] Gymnasium, and that was on the Gutenbergplatz.
Today that school is called the Gutenbergschule.
It is not called the [GERMAN] Gymnasium anymore.
Incidentally, that was the same school that my father attended, although the school at that time was at a different location.
In fact, my father and I had the same teacher, my father when he was a rather young man.
And when I had him in mathematics, he was ready for retirement.
In fact, he did retire after the first year or so that I had him.
He was extremely strict teacher and he also was a great protagonist of the cane, the [GERMAN].
And we got to feel it very often.
He also gave us slaps on the face by holding us one ear and hitting us with the other.
And my father remembered the same kind of punishment by the teacher.
But nothing was thought of it at that time.
That was considered part of the educational process.
The first time I actually became aware of anti-Semitism was-- was in-- once while I was still in the elementary school, and that was the day of the boycott.
I thought it was the first boycott, but it happened in, I believe, August of 1933.
I went to school as usual.
I did not know of any-- anything that was happening.
But when we were dismissed, Herr [?
Kanto, ?] our teacher, he came up to me and he said, I suggest you go right home.
Don't dawdle on the way and don't get involved in any conversations.
And I was very much surprised because I usually went right home.
I may have-- may have walked together with somebody else and we may have stopped to meet somebody, but usually when I-- but he never said that before.
And I didn't know why he would say that.
And I started to walk home.
And shortly before we got to the house, there was a Jewish butcher store.
And I noticed there was a crowd in front of the Jewish butcher store.
And also next to the butcher store was a box that displayed the [GERMAN], which was [GERMAN] anti-Semitic paper.
I don't-- can't call it a newspaper.
It was a hate-- hate sheet more than anything else, with a very bad cartoons of caricatures of Jews and a Jew baiting paper.
And people were gathered around that, as well.
In the past, I looked at that box occasionally and my mother had already told me, she said, if any people around that box, try to stay away from them.
That paper is not- that box has some very-- not very nice things written on it.
I was curious once and I did stop by and looked at it.
And I thought the whole thing was rather silly, I didn't pay much attention to it.
But when I got to the butcher store on the way home, I noticed that there was a Star of David was scrawled on there and Juden and placards were carried about saying, don't buy from Jews and so on.
And I sort of walked around that group.
I looked and there were some people pointing at me but didn't say anything.
I got home and my mother was at the door.
And she took me in-- in her arms and said, thank God you're home.
And then I said, what's going on?
I said, oh there is Jewish-- all the Jewish stores are being boycotted.
And as she talked to me, the phone rang.
And she said, oh my God.
I'll be right there.
And she grabbed me and she said, we have to go someplace.
She called a taxi and a taxi came and we went to a store, a department store, in the center of town that was called Blumenthal's department store.
And in the car, my mother mentioned something to the effect-- she didn't know exactly what happened-- she says-- said a friend of ours, a good friend of ours, Paul Davis by name, is in trouble.
We have to go there and see what we can do.
We went to the-- the taxi stopped in front of the store and there were people there with signs [GERMAN], don't buy from Jews.
My mother ignored them and she took me along into the store and somebody [INAUDIBLE] into an office and told my mother-- I was right there-- that they arrested Paul.
What happened was a woman bumped into him and claimed that he, I don't know, accosted her or did something to her and he has been arrested.
So I said, well, what-- what we have to do is we have to go and see his wife and family.
And she took a taxi again, we went up to their house, walked up a flight of steps.
Eva, who was the oldest sister, opened the door for us and she was white as a sheet.
And my mother said, what are you doing?
She said, we're packing.
Said, where are you going?
She said, I don't know.
And then the mother went inside-- the mother was there and said, well, I'm packing.
We have to go someplace.
So she said, come stay with us.
She said, no, I can't do that.
We're gonna-- I-- I think I'm gonna go to I don't know where she went, to some relatives somewhere else, I think out of town.
So she said, at least leave the children with us.
So she said, well, I want Eva to come along.
She said, Susie, the younger one, can stay with you if you want to.
And we took her home, she stayed with us for quite a while, I don't know for how.
And by the way, Susie and I and we are very-- to this day very good friends.
They live in Jersey and we visit each other constantly.
And well, that's-- we're still good friends, let's put it that way.
[LAUGHS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she stayed with us.
And Paul, I remember a story about him.
When he came out of [GERMAN], they called it, protective custody-- I don't know how long he was there, I think a few weeks-- and he was let go again.
And when he came out, he said to pass the time, they played chess and made chess figures out of bread, the soft part of the bread.
That I remember.
And shortly thereafter, they left for Portugal and the children actually were brought up by nuns in Portugal.
And after-- was it before the war or after the war-- they came to the United States.
I forget when they came here.
That was actually the first [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] experience that I had regarding any kind of anti-Semitism.
And that was during the time of the public school.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
Gee, that half hour went fast.
Did I remember too much?
It wasn't too much?
